National-level **elections in Italy** are called periodically to form a [parliament](/wiki/Parliament_of_Italy "Parliament of Italy") consisting of [two houses](/wiki/Bicameralism "Bicameralism"): the [Chamber of Deputies](/wiki/Chamber_of_Deputies_of_Italy "Chamber of Deputies of Italy") (*Camera dei Deputati*) with 400 members; and the [Senate of the Republic](/wiki/Italian_Senate "Italian Senate") (*Senato della Repubblica*) with 200 elected members, plus a few appointed [senators for life](/wiki/Senators_for_life "Senators for life"). Italy is a [parliamentary republic](/wiki/Parliamentary_republic "Parliamentary republic"): the [President of the Republic](/wiki/List_of_Presidents_of_the_Italian_Republic "List of Presidents of the Italian Republic") is elected for a seven-year term by the two houses of Parliament in joint session, together with special electors appointed by the Regional Councils.

Italy had a [mixed-member majoritarian](/wiki/Mixed-member_majoritarian_representation "Mixed-member majoritarian representation") system in place between 1993 and 2005.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1) The 2006 elections were the first elections conducted under a closed-list proportional system rather than a mixed system.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)

The most recent Italian general election was held on [25 September 2022](/wiki/2022_Italian_general_election "2022 Italian general election").

## 2022 general election

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Elections_in_Italy&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: 2022 general election")]

The last general election was held on 25 September 2022.

The [centre-right coalition](/wiki/Centre-right_coalition_(Italy) "Centre-right coalition (Italy)"), in which [Giorgia Meloni](/wiki/Giorgia_Meloni "Giorgia Meloni")'s [Brothers of Italy](/wiki/Brothers_of_Italy "Brothers of Italy") emerged as the main political force, won an [absolute majority](/wiki/Absolute_majority "Absolute majority") of seats in the Chamber of Deputies and in the Senate of the Republic. The [centre-left coalition](/wiki/Centre-left_coalition_(Italy) "Centre-left coalition (Italy)"), the anti-establishment [Five Star Movement](/wiki/Five_Star_Movement "Five Star Movement") and the centrist [Action - Italia Viva](/wiki/Action_-_Italia_Viva "Action - Italia Viva") came in second, third and fourth respectively.

## Referendums

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Elections_in_Italy&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Referendums")]

Main article: [Referendums in Italy](/wiki/Referendums_in_Italy "Referendums in Italy")

### Overview

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Elections_in_Italy&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Overview")]

The constitution of [Italy](/wiki/Italy "Italy") provides for two kinds of binding referendums.

A legislative referendum can be called in order to abrogate a law totally or partially, if requested by 500,000 electors or five regional councils. This kind of referendum is valid only if at least a majority of electors goes to the polling station. It is forbidden to call a referendum regarding financial laws or laws relating to pardons or the ratification of international treaties.

A constitutional referendum can be called in order to approve a constitutional law or amendment only when it has been approved by the Houses ([Chamber of Deputies](/wiki/Italian_Chamber_of_Deputies "Italian Chamber of Deputies") and [Senate of the Republic](/wiki/Italian_Senate "Italian Senate")) with a majority of less than two thirds in both or either House, and only at the request of one fifth of the members of either House, or 500,000 electors or five [Regional Councils](/wiki/Regional_Council_(Italy) "Regional Council (Italy)"). A constitutional referendum is valid no matter how many electors go to the polling station. Any citizen entitled to vote in an election may participate in a referendum.
